doc_name: FDA Aligns U.S. Medical Device Rules with Global Standards

chunks.doc_name: FDA Aligns U.S. Medical Device Rules with Global Standards
chunks.text: * [Regulatory & Quality](/regulatory-quality) * [News](/latest-news) # Medical Device Companies Must Navigate Historic FDA Rule Change A new FDA rule aligning U.S. medical device quality management standards with international requirements went into effect February 2, giving inspectors unprecedented access to previously protected company records while emphasizing risk management and quality culture. [![Picture of Claire Wallace](https://eu-images.contentstack.com/v3/assets/blt14ac89070d5e4751/blt8584ea235e86fe2d/68712238a851991bd7714901/headshot.jpeg?width=100&auto=webp&quality=80&disable=upscale "Picture of Claire Wallace")](/author/claire-wallace) [Claire Wallace,](/author/claire-wallace)Senior Writer,Informa Markets – Engineering February 10, 2026 13 Min Read ![](https://eu-images.contentstack.com/v3/assets/blt14ac89070d5e4751/blt9a6450e4ecba8501/698b8d45feba4452b9be5e12/qms_to_use.png?width=1280&auto=webp&quality=80&format=jpg&disable=upscale) cagkansayin via istock/getty images On Monday, February 2, a new final rule from FDA changing QMS requirements for medical devices went into effect. Manufacturers have had over two years to comply with the new rule, which is now full steam ahead. The change brings U.S. quality management system requirements in line with international standards, clarifies existing obligations for device manufacturers operating in the U.S. and globally, and more. The new QMS [amends the device current good manufacturing practice](https://www.fda.gov/medical-devices/postmarket-requirements-devices/quality-management-system-regulation-qmsr#:~:text=QMSR%20Inspections,-The%20FDA%20has&text=On%20February%202%2C%202026%2C%20the%20FDA%20stopped%20using%20the%20Quality,Device%20Manufacturers%20Compliance%20Program%3A%207382.850.) (CGMP) requirements of 21 CFR Part 820, incorporating by reference the international standard specific for medical device quality management systems set by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), ISO 13485:2016 Medical devices – Quality management systems – Requirements for regulatory purposes. It also aims to harmonize FDA’s CGMP regulatory framework with that used by other regulatory authorities. After February 2, 2026, FDA will no longer use the Inspection of Medical Device Manufacturers (7382.845) and Medical Device PMA Preapproval and PMA Postmarket Inspections (7383.001). Related:[FDA Adds New Submission Category in Human Factors Guidance Shake-Up](/regulatory-quality/fda-adds-new-submission-category-in-human-factors-guidance-shake-up) Following the rule change, MD+DI connected with Dennis Gucciardo, a partner at Morgan Lewis and expert on counseling domestic and global medical device manufacturers to help ensure they operate in compliance with FDA regulations, requirements, and expectations. Gucciardo has spent years working with companies ranging from small startups to large multinational corporations throughout the product life cycle, helping bring novel technologies to market while maintaining compliance and avoiding FDA enforcement actions. He connected with us to discuss the new QMS standards, how they align with international requirements, and what this means for all medical device manufacturers, from major corporations to small startups. ## What does "alignment with international QMS standards" actually mean in practice for U.S. device manufacturers who have been operating under the current CGMP framework for decades? Gucciardo: Taking a step back, and looking at the onus of the QMSR, if you are a U.S. operating company operating globally, how can we reduce the burden in terms of different requirements we have to meet? When you go back to the history of the QSR, which was released in 1996, shortly thereafter came 1345. 1345’s parent is 9001. 9001 is a system regulation for any company. A newspaper organization can be 9001 compliant. Any sort of product manufacturing can become 9001 compliant. 1345 then takes 9001 and applies it to device companies. QSR was based on 9001 at the time it existed. Then came 1345 after the fact. Over time, 1345 became the more accepted international standard. I am a U.S. lawyer, but if you go to Europe or other jurisdictions, it does not say you must comply

chunks.doc_name: FDA Aligns U.S. Medical Device Rules with Global Standards
chunks.text: sort of product manufacturing can become 9001 compliant. 1345 then takes 9001 and applies it to device companies. QSR was based on 9001 at the time it existed. Then came 1345 after the fact. Over time, 1345 became the more accepted international standard. I am a U.S. lawyer, but if you go to Europe or other jurisdictions, it does not say you must comply with 1345. What happens in these jurisdictions is they say you have to demonstrate that your device is safe and effective, and commonly the way for doing that is through a certification of 1345. That has become the global standard. Related:[QMSR Compliance Challenges: Critical Implementation Gaps Manufacturers Must Fix](/regulatory-quality/qmsr-compliance-challenges-critical-implementation-gaps-manufacturers-must-fix) If you are compliant with 1345, you can demonstrate that your devices are safe and effective and you can get authorized in that marketplace. Here in the U.S., we never went that route. The QSR survived and maintained. In the 20teens, the theory was as companies became more global and their markets were less U.S. focused, how can we reduce the regulatory burden? What is interesting is the QSR is a regulation that is not necessarily designed for U.S. operations, it is designed to be a global player. If you've been a global manufacturer in the U.S., you've already likely had to meet 1345 because you were operating in those jurisdictions. If you are only a U.S. maker, then 1345 was never on your radar. By switching over to 1345, the theory is we can reduce regulatory burden for global operation by making sure we're all aligned. Secondly, for those companies already operating globally, this could ease some regulatory burden. When you think about the 1345 switch, FDA has been dreaming about becoming more uniform globally. If you think about it like a venn diagram, there was the QSR, then there was 1345. For the most part, there was a lot of overlap. The words may not have been the same, but there was lots of overlap. What happened overtime was it showed foreign regulatory bodies, let’s say you got audited for 1345 in the U.S., but the issues weren't the same, people would say well 1345 is not the same as QSR and there could be a reason for the distinction. Now that has evaporated with 1345 becoming a player. The goal here is to help ease the regulatory burden on device companies operating globally, knowing that you are not likely making a device just for the U.S. marketplace but for global marketplaces. Related:[FDA Device Fees Soar: Is Regulatory Expansion Justified?](/regulatory-quality/fda-device-fees-soar-is-regulatory-expansion-justified-) ## The rule took effect Monday, and companies had a two year warning to fully comply. What should manufacturers be doing in these first 90 days to set themselves up for success? Gucciardo: FDA went dormant in January with inspections, because they were training all of their inspectors on 1345 and getting ready. I just had my first client tell me about their first inspection findings, and unsurprisingly one of the most explicit differences, though meaningfully there was no difference, is one of the things that underlines

chunks.doc_name: FDA Aligns U.S. Medical Device Rules with Global Standards
chunks.text: first 90 days to set themselves up for success? Gucciardo: FDA went dormant in January with inspections, because they were training all of their inspectors on 1345 and getting ready. I just had my first client tell me about their first inspection findings, and unsurprisingly one of the most explicit differences, though meaningfully there was no difference, is one of the things that underlines 1345 is explicitly risk management, and how you have to involve risk management in your quality systems. If you go to the QSR, there is one provision of design validation that talks about risk management in the framework of that, but that’s it. It is not in other areas of the QSR. There are some references in CAPA, but that's one of the big changes is the concept that FDA is explicitly looking at risk. The reality is they have been doing this for years, and I think in the last several years this has become more apparent. Especially with MDSAP. MDSAP allowed medical device companies that were compliant to avoid FDA inspections. What happened was, everyone signed up for it, because who wouldn’t want to avoid FDA inspection, but it did not bring the inspectorate in the U.S. down, the number of investigators largely stayed flat. These people have to go places. You are not going to lose your resources because you have nowhere to send them. So we started seeing more directed inspections typically based on recalls. Mainly, we started seeing an increase where if you had a class I recall, a directed inspection would follow and that inspection would be really focused on risk management. What the QMSR does is says, that is already happening, and if you look at the new FDA diagram in their compliance policy guidelines for how they're enforcing QMSR, the new diagram has risk management at the center, and how all of the quality systems feed into that. It is really pushing the concept that we want to see risk management built into decision making. FDA was already expecting this, and I saw it in many inspections, but now they have hooked risk management into the standard. That is a big change. My one client that gave me a heads up that they had an inspection, it was supposed to be a surveillance inspection and in the preannouncement the investigator actually pointed out, we are going to be looking at risk management. So the two things I am getting from FDA are one, they want to see risk explicitly included, and they are really making that a core message. The other thing is, FDA wants to see quality culture, and they want to see how it operates. If you look at ISO, and even QSR, you have these procedures to do things. So many observations and problems were, it’s not like companies did not have a procedure, everyone was already able to check the box. But one thing I get frustrated with is execution, and how companies are actually executing their procedures. That is this concept of quality culture; do

chunks.doc_name: FDA Aligns U.S. Medical Device Rules with Global Standards
chunks.text: operates. If you look at ISO, and even QSR, you have these procedures to do things. So many observations and problems were, it’s not like companies did not have a procedure, everyone was already able to check the box. But one thing I get frustrated with is execution, and how companies are actually executing their procedures. That is this concept of quality culture; do companies actually have the right resources, do they have the right people, are they trained, are they empowered to make the right decisions? As we think about QMSR and where FDA will focus the first year, it is going to be on those two concepts. I think one other thing that will be interesting and new to investigators and for years they have wanted this, is one of the things the QMSR does is it no longer provides protection during inspections for manual review, internal audit, and supplier audits. One of the things the QSR did was it had a protection that allowed for open conversation letting FDA look at records to quell conversation. That protection is gone. My view is that this is a forward looking issue. Companies should make sure they protect their legacy records, but I think one of the things that will be really different going forward is the ability to see this, and seeing how management is thinking and how they are getting to this quality culture piece. It is going to be an interesting dynamic moving forward because FDA has always wanted to pin issues on management, but they have not been given access to the records to make the case. That will be a change. Hopefully it will put more pressure on management to make sure they make the right decisions, but secondly, what we document, how we document it, and how these records are used against companies. ## You've handled numerous FDA warning letters and 483 observations. How do you expect this rule change to impact the most common compliance issues we've been seeing for the past 20 years? Gucciardo: I still think we'll have the same problems in design, CAPA, and complaint handling. I do not see a change there. One thing that is interesting is that 1345 has more requirements. Specifically, one change no one is thinking about is that you are supposed to protect patient information. One area we commonly get patient information is through complaint handling. If a manufacturer gets a complaint, someone got injured, there is information in there. Most medical device manufacturers are not governed by HIPAA, so they aren't subject to those rules, but 1345 means we have to protect this information. Companies will have to think differently about if they have complaints coming in, who will protect that patient information? That will be an interesting route to look at. Overall we will see the same stuff from CAPA, but we may see an uptick in management review because now they have access to these records. FDA for years has been wanting to see how management thinks and operates, and if they look

chunks.doc_name: FDA Aligns U.S. Medical Device Rules with Global Standards
chunks.text: if they have complaints coming in, who will protect that patient information? That will be an interesting route to look at. Overall we will see the same stuff from CAPA, but we may see an uptick in management review because now they have access to these records. FDA for years has been wanting to see how management thinks and operates, and if they look at these records and see management is not acting or operating correctly, they will use that. Under the old regime, all they could do was see if you were holding a management review, were the right people getting reviewed, but they couldn’t look at content. Companies should not mess up on management review, but now they can look at content and substance. So we may see some more flavors of management not acting within the quality system, which is a whole new level of findings. ## Looking ahead, how do you see this alignment affecting the timeline and strategy for bringing new medical devices to market, particularly for the startups versus established players you counsel? Gucciardo: Established players wanted this, and it came. So the one thing everyone will have to do is make sure their terminology and procedures meet the QSMR language. That is one thing I have been doing a lot; fixing our quality agreements because so many of them use QSR language and that’s no longer applicable. For startups, if you are new, you still need a quality system. Could 1345 be easier for them? Probably, because it is more explicit. New companies struggle with looking at regulation, because regulation says you have to do three basic things and otherwise it's up to you, off to the races. Sometimes companies struggle with competence, wondering if their procedures are good enough, if they have covered all of the right things. 1345 gives a bit more detail on what is expected. The concept of risk management will be new, so companies could struggle with that. What FDA is looking at is patient health and safety. I think startups can't just play the roles of clinician, consult, etc. They need clinicians on staff or consulting who can make legitimate decisions on patient health safety. Because those are going to be explicitly reviewed. I often work with startups who have an engineer, a quality person, and then that’s it. Where is the clinician who will add value to the situation? That is something companies will want to think about. ## Is there anything else you want to expand on? Gucciardo: I think what's really going to be fascinating moving forward as this continues to roll out, we'll get our first inspections that will come out but we won’t see enforcement action for probably another six months. What will be interesting is how U.S. device companies, previously if there was a bad FDA result, foreign bodies weren't concerned because there was a mindset that 1345 was not the QSR. Now they are all the same. If FDA comes in to inspect someone, and they have a list of observations under

chunks.doc_name: FDA Aligns U.S. Medical Device Rules with Global Standards
chunks.text: but we won’t see enforcement action for probably another six months. What will be interesting is how U.S. device companies, previously if there was a bad FDA result, foreign bodies weren't concerned because there was a mindset that 1345 was not the QSR. Now they are all the same. If FDA comes in to inspect someone, and they have a list of observations under 1345, and the notified body does the same thing, it'll put pressure on notified bodies to up their game to not feel as if they are not being as thorough as FDA would. The reality is, FDA is not going to give up base inspection authority. Having a government regulator look at you versus a notified body is a different level of expertise and understanding. I am sure there will be statistics about “notified bodies are finding these level of findings,” versus “FDA is showing these level of findings.” I think it will put pressure on notified bodies to really do something. It will be really awkward when FDA finds things that they are not. That is going to come into play as we see this rollout. So companies shouldn’t be surprised if notified bodies increase their game. I think companies need to be careful about records, and records FDA can now see and what they have access to and don't. Especially for the first year. FDA inspections are retrospective. So if we think about the first month, FDA can say okay it’s February 6, I would like to see one of your records generated from QSR, but there's only been six days of records. If they pull records from December 2025, that does not comply with QSMR. It'll be fits and starts until we fully switch over. Usually inspections are a two-year lookback. There could be some interesting struggles because if FDA looks at a record from December, there could be legit differences between procedures and how the records execute. I think we have to be ready for making sure FDA understands OK, this record was from the QSR, this is QMSR, let’s make sure we have our roads in play. I see that as an issue until we can smooth this out. I don’t expect leniency in the first year, as FDA has been adamant that come February 2, it’s go time. It’s full game and companies need to meet all requirements. But I do think this transition naturally will have kinks because inspections are retrospective, but they are looking at it under a new light. It will be fun to see the first warning letters, and how FDA writes the findings. ## About the Author [![Claire Wallace](https://eu-images.contentstack.com/v3/assets/blt14ac89070d5e4751/blt8584ea235e86fe2d/68712238a851991bd7714901/headshot.jpeg?width=400&auto=webp&quality=80&disable=upscale "Claire Wallace")](/author/claire-wallace) [Claire Wallace](/author/claire-wallace) Senior Writer, Informa Markets – Engineering Claire Wallace is a senior staff writer at Medical Device + Diagnostics Industry (MD+DI), where she covers key developments in the healthcare and medical device industry. With nearly a decade of journalism experience, she brings a deep understanding of the healthcare industry and a proven ability to connect with physicians, medtech leaders, and policy experts on the issues

chunks.doc_name: FDA Aligns U.S. Medical Device Rules with Global Standards
chunks.text: Senior Writer, Informa Markets – Engineering Claire Wallace is a senior staff writer at Medical Device + Diagnostics Industry (MD+DI), where she covers key developments in the healthcare and medical device industry. With nearly a decade of journalism experience, she brings a deep understanding of the healthcare industry and a proven ability to connect with physicians, medtech leaders, and policy experts on the issues shaping care delivery today. She is committed to delivering nuanced coverage that helps industry professionals anticipate trends, understand regulatory shifts, and identify opportunities in an increasingly complex healthcare ecosystem. Before joining MD+DI, Claire spent three years at Becker’s Healthcare as a writer and assistant editor, leading coverage on developments across orthopedics, outpatient care, cardiology, and gastroenterology that bridged perspectives from clinicians and healthcare executives. Her reporting background also includes lifestyle, political, and clean energy news, with bylines in various national and digital publications. Claire earned her bachelor’s degree in Communications and Media & Screen Studies from Northeastern University, where she also minored in Political Science and Journalism. She holds a master’s degree in Media Advocacy and is currently based in Chicago. [See more from Claire Wallace](/author/claire-wallace) Sign up for the QMED & MD+DI Daily newsletter.
